  gErrors <- mokken::check.errors(dall[,c("liking", "vivid", "origin", "aesth", "curios")], returnGplus=TRUE, returnOplus=TRUE); # needs 128Gb+ RAM!
  saveRDS(gErrors, file=xzfile("./cache/gErrors.rds", compression=9));
} else
{
  gErrors <- readRDS(xzfile("./cache/gErrors.rds"));
}
gPlus   <- gErrors$Gplus;
oPlus   <- gErrors$Oplus;
hist(gPlus, main="Histogram of gPlus", xlab="gPlus (# of Guttman errors)");
# cor(cbind(oPlus, gPlus, xPlus))

# identify the outliers:
Q3 <- summary(gPlus)[[5]];
IQR <- Q3 - summary(gPlus)[[2]];
outlier <- gPlus > Q3 + 1.5 * IQR;
# if needed to further analyse outliers
# cbind(mydata, gPlus)[outlier,]
# then possible sensitivity analysis:
# coefH(d_tone_wide[!outlier, -1])
```

There were `r sum(outlier)` cases (out of `r length(gPlus)`, i.e. `r round(100*sum(outlier)/length(gPlus), 1)`%) with a number of Guttman errors bigger than (Q3 + 1.5*IQR) = `r Q3 + 1.5*IQR`. 

```{r, warning=FALSE, message=FALSE, echo=FALSE, results='hide'}
# Compute scalability coefficients
if( !file.exists("./cache/Hvalues.rds") ) # computationally expensive
{
  Hvalues_full <- mokken::coefH(dall[,c("liking", "vivid", "origin", "aesth", "curios")], se=TRUE, ci=0.95);
  Hvalues <- list("Hi"=Hvalues_full$Hi, "H"=Hvalues_full$H); # keep only the info we actually need, as the full object is way too big
  saveRDS(Hvalues, file=xzfile("./cache/Hvalues.rds", compression=9));
} else
{
  Hvalues <- readRDS(xzfile("./cache/Hvalues.rds"));
}

# examine aisp for increasing c levels (run the function you defined above and give it a name)
if( !file.exists("./cache/motable.rds") ) # computationally expensive
{
  motable <- moscales.for.lowerbounds(dall[,c("liking", "vivid", "origin", "aesth", "curios")]);
  motable <- cbind("Items"=rownames(motable), motable);
  saveRDS(motable, file=xzfile("./cache/motable.rds", compression=9));
} else
{
  motable <- readRDS(xzfile("./cache/motable.rds"));
}

# if you need to view it
# View(aispmydata)
```

```{r results = 'asis', echo=FALSE}
d <- as.data.frame(cbind("Items"=rownames(Hvalues$Hi), Hvalues$Hi)); names(d) <- c("item", "H", "se", "95CI");
d$H <- as.numeric(trimws(d$H));
knitr::kable(d, row.names=FALSE, align="r", col.names=c("Items", "Item H", "se", "95% ci"),
             caption=capTab("MSA: Homogeneity values (with standard errors and 95%CIs) for all items, sorted by item name."))
```

The complete item set has a homogeneity value *H* (se, 95%CI) of `r Hvalues$H`: this is significantly higher than the recommended 0.30, suggesting that the scale *is homogeneous*.
This is further supported by the fact that all items have a homogeneity above this value.

```{r results = 'asis', echo=FALSE}
knitr::kable(motable, row.names=FALSE, align="r",
             caption=capTab("MSA: *aisp* for increasing H thresholds (c) for all items."));
```

These results suggest that these items most probably form a single scale.


### PCA and FA

#### Overall

```{r include=FALSE}
# PCA and EFA across trials:
d <- dall[,c("liking", "vivid", "origin", "aesth", "curios")];

# PCA:
t_pca <- prcomp(d, scale.=TRUE, center=TRUE);
t_pca;
summary(t_pca);

# EFA:
# Tests of factorability (see https://towardsdatascience.com/exploratory-factor-analysis-in-r-e31b0015f224):
psych::KMO(r=cor(d)); # Kaiser-Meyer-Olkin (KMO) sampling adequacy = 0.88 >> 0.60 -> ok
psych::cortest.bartlett(d); # p=0 -> ok
det(cor(d)); # seems fine

# Number of factors:
psych::fa.parallel(d, fa="fa", n.iter=100, error.bars=TRUE); # -> suggests 3 factors, but this is way too many for just 5 variables
psych::nfactors(d); # -> 1 or 2 factors

t_fa_1f <- factanal(d, 1, rotation="promax"); t_fa_1f; psych::fa.diagram(t_fa_1f$loadings); # p-value is 0 
t_fa_2f <- factanal(d, 2, rotation="promax"); t_fa_2f; psych::fa.diagram(t_fa_2f$loadings); # p-value is 5.07e-23
```

For **PCA**, PC~1~ explains `r round(100*(s <- summary(t_pca)$importance)["Proportion of Variance", "PC1"],1)`% of the variance, followed by PC~2~ which explains `r round(100*(s <- summary(t_pca)$importance)["Proportion of Variance", "PC2"],1)`%, suggesting there is a main factor on which all items load, but PC2 also seems to say something about the structure of the questions:

```{r fig.width=5, fig.height=4, fig.cap=capFig("Screeplot of the PCA of all the tone items together."), warning=FALSE}
fviz_eig(t_pca);
```
```{r fig.width=11, fig.height=10, fig.cap=capFig("Loading of the tone items on the first 2 PCs."), warning=FALSE}
fviz_pca_var(t_pca,
             col.var = "contrib", # Color by contributions to the PC
             gradient.cols = c("#00AFBB", "#E7B800", "#FC4E07"),
             repel = TRUE     # Avoid text overlapping
             );
```

The actual loadings on the first two PCs are:
```{r}
kable(round(t_pca$rotation[,1:2],2));
```


For **EFA**, all the preliminary tests suggest that factor analysis is appropriate (`r sprintf("Kaiser-Meyer-Olkin = %.2f > 0.60; Bartlett's test is significant: *χ*^2^(%d)=%.1f, *p*=%.3g; and det(cor(data))=%.2g > 0", psych::KMO(r=cor(d))$MSA, (tmp <- psych::cortest.bartlett(d))$df, tmp$chisq, tmp$p.value, det(cor(d)))`).
The suggested number of factors seems to be 1 or 2 factors:

```{r fig.width=4, fig.height=4, fig.cap=capFig("Screeplot of the observed, simulated and randomized data with 1 standard deviation error bars (as generated by `fa.parallel())`."), results='hide'}
fa.parallel(d, fa="fa", n.iter=100, error.bars=TRUE);
```
```{r fig.width=4*2, fig.height=4*2, fig.cap=capFig("Number of factors as suggested by the VSS criterion (top left), the complexity of the solution (top right), BIC (bottom left) and Root Mean Residual (bottom right), as implemented by `nfactors()`."), results='hide'}
nfactors(d);
```

The two FA models are (but the fit to the data is pretty bad): 
```{r fig.width=6, fig.height=4, fig.cap=capFig("Loadings of the variables in the 2-factors model."), results='hide'}
fa.diagram(t_fa_1f$loadings);
```
```{r fig.width=6, fig.height=4, fig.cap=capFig("Loadings of the variables in the 2-factors model."), results='hide'}
fa.diagram(t_fa_2f$loadings);
```
and the actual loadings on the two factors are (showing only those ≥ 0.1 in absolute value):
```{r}
t_fa_1f$loadings;
t_fa_2f$loadings;
```

Let's look at this separately for each type of judge...


#### Human judges only

```{r include=FALSE}
# PCA and EFA across trials:
d.hum <- dall[dall$jtyp=="human",c("liking", "vivid", "origin", "aesth", "curios")];

# PCA:
t_pca.hum <- prcomp(d.hum, scale.=TRUE, center=TRUE);
t_pca.hum;
summary(t_pca.hum);

# EFA:
# Tests of factorability (see https://towardsdatascience.com/exploratory-factor-analysis-in-r-e31b0015f224):
psych::KMO(r=cor(d.hum)); # Kaiser-Meyer-Olkin (KMO) sampling adequacy = 0.91 >> 0.60 -> ok
psych::cortest.bartlett(d.hum); # p=0 -> ok
det(cor(d.hum)); # seems fine

# Number of factors:
psych::fa.parallel(d.hum, fa="fa", n.iter=100, error.bars=TRUE); # -> suggests 3 factors, but this is way too many for just 5 variables
psych::nfactors(d.hum); # -> 1 or 2 factors

t_fa_1f.hum <- factanal(d.hum, 1, rotation="promax"); t_fa_1f.hum; psych::fa.diagram(t_fa_1f.hum$loadings); # p-value is 0 
t_fa_2f.hum <- factanal(d.hum, 2, rotation="promax"); t_fa_2f.hum; psych::fa.diagram(t_fa_2f.hum$loadings); # p-value is 6.3e-74
```

For **PCA**, PC~1~ explains `r round(100*(s <- summary(t_pca.hum)$importance)["Proportion of Variance", "PC1"],1)`% of the variance, followed by PC~2~ which explains `r round(100*(s <- summary(t_pca.hum)$importance)["Proportion of Variance", "PC2"],1)`%, suggesting there is a main factor on which all items load, but PC2 also seems to say something about the structure of the questions:

```{r fig.width=5, fig.height=4, fig.cap=capFig("Screeplot of the PCA of all the tone items together."), warning=FALSE}
fviz_eig(t_pca.hum);
```
```{r fig.width=11, fig.height=10, fig.cap=capFig("Loading of the tone items on the first 2 PCs."), warning=FALSE}
fviz_pca_var(t_pca.hum,
             col.var = "contrib", # Color by contributions to the PC
             gradient.cols = c("#00AFBB", "#E7B800", "#FC4E07"),
             repel = TRUE     # Avoid text overlapping
             );
```

The actual loadings on the first two PCs are:
```{r}
kable(round(t_pca.hum$rotation[,1:2],2));
```


For **EFA**, all the preliminary tests suggest that factor analysis is appropriate (`r sprintf("Kaiser-Meyer-Olkin = %.2f > 0.60; Bartlett's test is significant: *χ*^2^(%d)=%.1f, *p*=%.3g; and det(cor(data))=%.2g > 0", psych::KMO(r=cor(d.hum))$MSA, (tmp <- psych::cortest.bartlett(d.hum))$df, tmp$chisq, tmp$p.value, det(cor(d.hum)))`).
The suggested number of factors seems to be 1 or 2 factors:

```{r fig.width=4, fig.height=4, fig.cap=capFig("Screeplot of the observed, simulated and randomized data with 1 standard deviation error bars (as generated by `fa.parallel())`."), results='hide'}
fa.parallel(d.hum, fa="fa", n.iter=100, error.bars=TRUE);
```
```{r fig.width=4*2, fig.height=4*2, fig.cap=capFig("Number of factors as suggested by the VSS criterion (top left), the complexity of the solution (top right), BIC (bottom left) and Root Mean Residual (bottom right), as implemented by `nfactors()`."), results='hide'}
nfactors(d.hum);
```

The two FA models are (but the fit to the data is pretty bad): 
```{r fig.width=6, fig.height=4, fig.cap=capFig("Loadings of the variables in the 2-factors model."), results='hide'}
fa.diagram(t_fa_1f.hum$loadings);
```
```{r fig.width=6, fig.height=4, fig.cap=capFig("Loadings of the variables in the 2-factors model."), results='hide'}
fa.diagram(t_fa_2f.hum$loadings);
```
and the actual loadings on the two factors are (showing only those ≥ 0.1 in absolute value):
```{r}
t_fa_1f.hum$loadings;
t_fa_2f.hum$loadings;
```


#### AI judges only

```{r include=FALSE}
# PCA and EFA across trials:
d.ai <- dall[dall$jtyp=="AI",c("liking", "vivid", "origin", "aesth", "curios")];

# PCA:
t_pca.ai <- prcomp(d.ai, scale.=TRUE, center=TRUE);
t_pca.ai;
summary(t_pca.ai);

# EFA:
# Tests of factorability (see https://towardsdatascience.com/exploratory-factor-analysis-in-r-e31b0015f224):
psych::KMO(r=cor(d.ai)); # Kaiser-Meyer-Olkin (KMO) sampling adequacy = 0.82 >> 0.60 -> ok
psych::cortest.bartlett(d.ai); # p=0 -> ok
det(cor(d.ai)); # seems fine

# Number of factors:
psych::fa.parallel(d.ai, fa="fa", n.iter=100, error.bars=TRUE); # -> suggests 2 factors
psych::nfactors(d.ai); # -> 1 or 2 factors

t_fa_1f.ai <- factanal(d.ai, 1, rotation="promax"); t_fa_1f.ai; psych::fa.diagram(t_fa_1f.ai$loadings); # p-value is 0 
t_fa_2f.ai <- factanal(d.ai, 2, rotation="promax"); t_fa_2f.ai; psych::fa.diagram(t_fa_2f.ai$loadings); # p-value is 4.93e-29
```

For **PCA**, PC~1~ explains `r round(100*(s <- summary(t_pca.ai)$importance)["Proportion of Variance", "PC1"],1)`% of the variance, followed by PC~2~ which explains `r round(100*(s <- summary(t_pca.ai)$importance)["Proportion of Variance", "PC2"],1)`%, suggesting there is a main factor on which all items load, but PC2 also seems to say something about the structure of the questions:

```{r fig.width=5, fig.height=4, fig.cap=capFig("Screeplot of the PCA of all the tone items together."), warning=FALSE}
fviz_eig(t_pca.ai);
```
```{r fig.width=11, fig.height=10, fig.cap=capFig("Loading of the tone items on the first 2 PCs."), warning=FALSE}
fviz_pca_var(t_pca.ai,
             col.var = "contrib", # Color by contributions to the PC
             gradient.cols = c("#00AFBB", "#E7B800", "#FC4E07"),
             repel = TRUE     # Avoid text overlapping
             );
```

The actual loadings on the first two PCs are:
```{r}
kable(round(t_pca.ai$rotation[,1:2],2));
```


For **EFA**, all the preliminary tests suggest that factor analysis is appropriate (`r sprintf("Kaiser-Meyer-Olkin = %.2f > 0.60; Bartlett's test is significant: *χ*^2^(%d)=%.1f, *p*=%.3g; and det(cor(data))=%.2g > 0", psych::KMO(r=cor(d.ai))$MSA, (tmp <- psych::cortest.bartlett(d.ai))$df, tmp$chisq, tmp$p.value, det(cor(d.ai)))`).
The suggested number of factors seems to be 1 or 2 factors:

```{r fig.width=4, fig.height=4, fig.cap=capFig("Screeplot of the observed, simulated and randomized data with 1 standard deviation error bars (as generated by `fa.parallel())`."), results='hide'}
fa.parallel(d.ai, fa="fa", n.iter=100, error.bars=TRUE);
```
```{r fig.width=4*2, fig.height=4*2, fig.cap=capFig("Number of factors as suggested by the VSS criterion (top left), the complexity of the solution (top right), BIC (bottom left) and Root Mean Residual (bottom right), as implemented by `nfactors()`."), results='hide'}
nfactors(d.ai);
```

The two FA models are (but the fit to the data is pretty bad): 
```{r fig.width=6, fig.height=4, fig.cap=capFig("Loadings of the variables in the 2-factors model."), results='hide'}
fa.diagram(t_fa_1f.ai$loadings);
```
```{r fig.width=6, fig.height=4, fig.cap=capFig("Loadings of the variables in the 2-factors model."), results='hide'}
fa.diagram(t_fa_2f.ai$loadings);
```
and the actual loadings on the two factors are (showing only those ≥ 0.1 in absolute value):
```{r}
t_fa_1f.ai$loadings;
t_fa_2f.ai$loadings;
```


#### Guided AI judges only

```{r include=FALSE}
# PCA and EFA across trials:
d.gai <- dall[dall$jtyp=="gAI",c("liking", "vivid", "origin", "aesth", "curios")];

# PCA:
t_pca.gai <- prcomp(d.gai, scale.=TRUE, center=TRUE);
t_pca.gai;
summary(t_pca.gai);

# EFA:
# Tests of factorability (see https://towardsdatascience.com/exploratory-factor-analysis-in-r-e31b0015f224):
psych::KMO(r=cor(d.gai)); # Kaiser-Meyer-Olkin (KMO) sampling adequacy = 0.90 >> 0.60 -> ok
psych::cortest.bartlett(d.gai); # p=0 -> ok
det(cor(d.gai)); # seems fine

# Number of factors:
psych::fa.parallel(d.gai, fa="fa", n.iter=100, error.bars=TRUE); # -> suggests 2 factors
psych::nfactors(d.gai); # -> 1 or 2 factors

t_fa_1f.gai <- factanal(d.gai, 1, rotation="promax"); t_fa_1f.gai; psych::fa.diagram(t_fa_1f.gai$loadings); # p-value is 0 
t_fa_2f.gai <- factanal(d.gai, 2, rotation="promax"); t_fa_2f.gai; psych::fa.diagram(t_fa_2f.gai$loadings); # p-value is 0.787
```

For **PCA**, PC~1~ explains `r round(100*(s <- summary(t_pca.gai)$importance)["Proportion of Variance", "PC1"],1)`% of the variance, followed by PC~2~ which explains `r round(100*(s <- summary(t_pca.gai)$importance)["Proportion of Variance", "PC2"],1)`%, suggesting there is a main factor on which all items load, but PC2 also seems to say something about the structure of the questions:

```{r fig.width=5, fig.height=4, fig.cap=capFig("Screeplot of the PCA of all the tone items together."), warning=FALSE}
fviz_eig(t_pca.gai);
```
```{r fig.width=11, fig.height=10, fig.cap=capFig("Loading of the tone items on the first 2 PCs."), warning=FALSE}
fviz_pca_var(t_pca.gai,
             col.var = "contrib", # Color by contributions to the PC
             gradient.cols = c("#00AFBB", "#E7B800", "#FC4E07"),
             repel = TRUE     # Avoid text overlapping
             );
```

The actual loadings on the first two PCs are:
```{r}
kable(round(t_pca.gai$rotation[,1:2],2));
```


For **EFA**, all the preliminary tests suggest that factor analysis is appropriate (`r sprintf("Kaiser-Meyer-Olkin = %.2f > 0.60; Bartlett's test is significant: *χ*^2^(%d)=%.1f, *p*=%.3g; and det(cor(data))=%.2g > 0", psych::KMO(r=cor(d.gai))$MSA, (tmp <- psych::cortest.bartlett(d.gai))$df, tmp$chisq, tmp$p.value, det(cor(d.gai)))`).
The suggested number of factors seems to be 1 or 2 factors:

```{r fig.width=4, fig.height=4, fig.cap=capFig("Screeplot of the observed, simulated and randomized data with 1 standard deviation error bars (as generated by `fa.parallel())`."), results='hide'}
fa.parallel(d.gai, fa="fa", n.iter=100, error.bars=TRUE);
```
```{r fig.width=4*2, fig.height=4*2, fig.cap=capFig("Number of factors as suggested by the VSS criterion (top left), the complexity of the solution (top right), BIC (bottom left) and Root Mean Residual (bottom right), as implemented by `nfactors()`."), results='hide'}
nfactors(d.gai);
```

The two FA models are (but the fit to the data is pretty bad): 
```{r fig.width=6, fig.height=4, fig.cap=capFig("Loadings of the variables in the 2-factors model."), results='hide'}
fa.diagram(t_fa_1f.gai$loadings);
```
```{r fig.width=6, fig.height=4, fig.cap=capFig("Loadings of the variables in the 2-factors model."), results='hide'}
fa.diagram(t_fa_2f.gai$loadings);
```
and the actual loadings on the two factors are (showing only those ≥ 0.1 in absolute value):
```{r}
t_fa_1f.gai$loadings;
t_fa_2f.gai$loadings;
```


#### Let's compare human, AI and guided AI judges

```{r results='hide'}
# See https://lavaan.ugent.be/tutorial/groups.html
if( !file.exists("./cache/1FA.rds") )
{
  crea.1f <- "
    crea =~ liking + vivid + origin + aesth + curios;
  ";
  
  # configurational invariance:
  crea.1f.fit <- cfa(crea.1f, data=dall, group="jtyp", se="bootstrap", test="bootstrap");
  summary(crea.1f.fit);
  fitMeasures(crea.1f.fit, c("chisq", "df", "pvalue", "cfi", "tli", "nnfi", "rfi"));
  lavaanPlot(model=crea.1f.fit, coefs=TRUE, sig=1.00, stand=TRUE, covs=TRUE, stars=c("regress", "latent", "covs"), edge_options=list(color="gray"));
  # weak invariance:
  crea.1f.fit.wi <- cfa(crea.1f, data=dall, group="jtyp", group.equal="loadings", se="bootstrap", test="bootstrap");
  # strong invariance:
  crea.1f.fit.si <- cfa(crea.1f, data=dall, group="jtyp", group.equal=c("intercepts", "loadings"), se="bootstrap", test="bootstrap");
  # model comparison tests
  lavTestLRT(crea.1f.fit, crea.1f.fit.wi, crea.1f.fit.si); # both p-values are signif. ->  factor loadings and latent means are different between judge types! 
  
  # pairwise comparisons between types of judges:
  # H vs AI:
  d <- dall[dall$jtyp %in% c("human","AI"),] %>% mutate(jtyp=as.character(jtyp));
  crea.1f.ha.fit <- cfa(crea.1f, data=d, group="jtyp", se="bootstrap", test="bootstrap");
  summary(crea.1f.ha.fit);
  fitMeasures(crea.1f.ha.fit, c("chisq", "df", "pvalue", "cfi", "tli", "nnfi", "rfi"));
  lavaanPlot(model=crea.1f.ha.fit, coefs=TRUE, sig=1.00, stand=TRUE, covs=TRUE, stars=c("regress", "latent", "covs"), edge_options=list(color="gray"));
  crea.1f.ha.fit.wi <- cfa(crea.1f, data=d, group="jtyp", group.equal="loadings", se="bootstrap", test="bootstrap");
  crea.1f.ha.fit.si <- cfa(crea.1f, data=d, group="jtyp", group.equal=c("intercepts", "loadings"), se="bootstrap", test="bootstrap");
  lavTestLRT(crea.1f.ha.fit, crea.1f.ha.fit.wi, crea.1f.ha.fit.si); # both p-values are signif. ->  factor loadings and latent means are different between judge types! 
  
  # H vs gAI:
  d <- dall[dall$jtyp %in% c("human","gAI"),] %>% mutate(jtyp=as.character(jtyp));
  crea.1f.hg.fit <- cfa(crea.1f, data=d, group="jtyp", se="bootstrap", test="bootstrap");
  summary(crea.1f.hg.fit);
  fitMeasures(crea.1f.hg.fit, c("chisq", "df", "pvalue", "cfi", "tli", "nnfi", "rfi"));
  lavaanPlot(model=crea.1f.hg.fit, coefs=TRUE, sig=1.00, stand=TRUE, covs=TRUE, stars=c("regress", "latent", "covs"), edge_options=list(color="gray"));
  crea.1f.hg.fit.wi <- cfa(crea.1f, data=d, group="jtyp", group.equal="loadings", se="bootstrap", test="bootstrap");
  crea.1f.hg.fit.si <- cfa(crea.1f, data=d, group="jtyp", group.equal=c("intercepts", "loadings"), se="bootstrap", test="bootstrap");
  lavTestLRT(crea.1f.hg.fit, crea.1f.hg.fit.wi, crea.1f.hg.fit.si); # both p-values are signif. ->  factor loadings and latent means are different between judge types! 
  
  # AI vs gAI:
  d <- dall[dall$jtyp %in% c("AI","gAI"),] %>% mutate(jtyp=as.character(jtyp));
  crea.1f.ag.fit <- cfa(crea.1f, data=d, group="jtyp", se="bootstrap", test="bootstrap");
  summary(crea.1f.ag.fit);
  fitMeasures(crea.1f.ag.fit, c("chisq", "df", "pvalue", "cfi", "tli", "nnfi", "rfi"));
  lavaanPlot(model=crea.1f.ag.fit, coefs=TRUE, sig=1.00, stand=TRUE, covs=TRUE, stars=c("regress", "latent", "covs"), edge_options=list(color="gray"));
  crea.1f.ag.fit.wi <- cfa(crea.1f, data=d, group="jtyp", group.equal="loadings", se="bootstrap", test="bootstrap");
  crea.1f.ag.fit.si <- cfa(crea.1f, data=d, group="jtyp", group.equal=c("intercepts", "loadings"), se="bootstrap", test="bootstrap");
  lavTestLRT(crea.1f.ag.fit, crea.1f.ag.fit.wi, crea.1f.ag.fit.si); # both p-values are signif. ->  factor loadings and latent means are different between judge types! 
  
  # separately for types of judges:
  crea.1f.fit.hum <- cfa(crea.1f, data=dall[dall$jtyp=="human",], se="bootstrap", test="bootstrap");
  summary(crea.1f.fit.hum);
  fitMeasures(crea.1f.fit.hum, c("chisq", "df", "pvalue", "cfi", "tli", "nnfi", "rfi"));
  lavaanPlot(model=crea.1f.fit.hum, coefs=TRUE, sig=1.00, stand=TRUE, covs=TRUE, stars=c("regress", "latent", "covs"), edge_options=list(color="gray"));
  
  crea.1f.fit.ai <- cfa(crea.1f, data=dall[dall$jtyp=="AI",], se="bootstrap", test="bootstrap");
  summary(crea.1f.fit.ai);
  fitMeasures(crea.1f.fit.ai, c("chisq", "df", "pvalue", "cfi", "tli", "nnfi", "rfi"));
  lavaanPlot(model=crea.1f.fit.ai, coefs=TRUE, sig=1.00, stand=TRUE, covs=TRUE, stars=c("regress", "latent", "covs"), edge_options=list(color="gray"));
  
  crea.1f.fit.gai <- cfa(crea.1f, data=dall[dall$jtyp=="gAI",], se="bootstrap", test="bootstrap");
  summary(crea.1f.fit.gai);
  fitMeasures(crea.1f.fit.gai, c("chisq", "df", "pvalue", "cfi", "tli", "nnfi", "rfi"));
  lavaanPlot(model=crea.1f.fit.gai, coefs=TRUE, sig=1.00, stand=TRUE, covs=TRUE, stars=c("regress", "latent", "covs"), edge_options=list(color="gray"));

  # collect and save these results:
  crea1FA <- list("model"=crea.1f, 
                  "fit"=crea.1f.fit, "invariance.tests"=lavTestLRT(crea.1f.fit, crea.1f.fit.wi, crea.1f.fit.si), 
                  "fit.h.ai"  =crea.1f.ha.fit, "invariance.tests.h.ai"=lavTestLRT(crea.1f.ha.fit, crea.1f.ha.fit.wi, crea.1f.ha.fit.si), 
                  "fit.h.gai" =crea.1f.hg.fit, "invariance.tests.h.gai"=lavTestLRT(crea.1f.hg.fit, crea.1f.hg.fit.wi, crea.1f.hg.fit.si), 
                  "fit.ai.gai"=crea.1f.ag.fit, "invariance.tests.ai.gai"=lavTestLRT(crea.1f.ag.fit, crea.1f.ag.fit.wi, crea.1f.ag.fit.si), 
                  "fit.hum"=crea.1f.fit.hum, "fit.ai"=crea.1f.fit.ai, "fit.gai"=crea.1f.fit.gai);
  saveRDS(crea1FA, file="./cache/1FA.rds", compress="xz");
} else
{
  crea1FA <- readRDS("./cache/1FA.rds");
}
```

We fitted a 1FA model to the whole data in a multi-group analysis with *jtyp* defining the three groups: humans vs AI judges vs guided AI judges.

The model is:
```{r fig.width=7, fig.height=2, fig.cap=capFig("1FA model fitted to the whole dataset but splitted by *jtyp*. There are three arrows and loadings for each indicator, the first being for the AI rater, the second for the human raters, and the third for the guided AI rater. The loadings are standardized and the stars represent their significance level.")}
lavaanPlot(model=crea1FA$fit, coefs=TRUE, sig=0.05, stand=TRUE, covs=TRUE, stars=c("regress", "latent", "covs"), edge_options=list(color="gray"));
```
and its summary (using bootstrap for *p*-values and tests):
```{r}
summary(crea1FA$fit, stand=TRUE);
```
It clearly does not fit the data very well (the χ^2^ test is highly significant) and the other indices of fit are not great either:
```{r}
fitMeasures(crea1FA$fit, c("chisq", "df", "pvalue", "cfi", "tli", "nnfi", "rfi"));
```

Let's check if the same model holds for the three types of judges or not, but checking its configurational, weak and strong invariance, respectively (see [`lavaan`'s multiple groups help](https://lavaan.ugent.be/tutorial/groups.html) for details):
```{r}
crea1FA$invariance.tests;
```
both tests are highly significant, meaning that both the factor loadings and the latent means are different between the types of judges. 


Focusing on each pairwise comparison between types of judges, we have:

Human vs AI:
```{r fig.width=7, fig.height=2, fig.cap=capFig("1FA model fitted to the whole dataset splitted AI (left) vs human (right).")}
lavaanPlot(model=crea1FA$fit.h.ai, coefs=TRUE, sig=0.05, stand=TRUE, covs=TRUE, stars=c("regress", "latent", "covs"), edge_options=list(color="gray"));
crea1FA$invariance.tests.h.ai;
```

Human vs gAI:
```{r fig.width=7, fig.height=2, fig.cap=capFig("1FA model fitted to the whole dataset splitted human (left) vs gAI (right).")}
lavaanPlot(model=crea1FA$fit.h.gai, coefs=TRUE, sig=0.05, stand=TRUE, covs=TRUE, stars=c("regress", "latent", "covs"), edge_options=list(color="gray"));
crea1FA$invariance.tests.h.gai;
```

AI vs gAI:
```{r fig.width=7, fig.height=2, fig.cap=capFig("1FA model fitted to the whole dataset splitted AI (left) vs gAI (right).")}
lavaanPlot(model=crea1FA$fit.ai.gai, coefs=TRUE, sig=0.05, stand=TRUE, covs=TRUE, stars=c("regress", "latent", "covs"), edge_options=list(color="gray"));
crea1FA$invariance.tests.ai.gai;
```

When fitted separately for each type of judge, the models still don't fit the data very well, but the fit indices do change:

- for humans judges:
```{r}
fitMeasures(crea1FA$fit.hum, c("chisq", "df", "pvalue", "cfi", "tli", "nnfi", "rfi"));
```

- for AI judge:
```{r}
fitMeasures(crea1FA$fit.ai, c("chisq", "df", "pvalue", "cfi", "tli", "nnfi", "rfi"));
```

- for guided AI judge:
```{r}
fitMeasures(crea1FA$fit.gai, c("chisq", "df", "pvalue", "cfi", "tli", "nnfi", "rfi"));
```

suggesting that the 1FA model fits the human and guided AI judgements much better than the AI ones.


#### Adding the 1FA estimates to the database

Nevertheless, in order to compare the two types of judges, we will use the 1FA model for both of them, but taking into account their differences, so we will add the estimated scores on this "creativity" scale (*crea*) back in the database:

```{r results='hide'}
crea1FA.pred <- lavPredict(crea1FA$fit, type="lv", method="regression", transform=TRUE, append.data=TRUE, assemble=TRUE);
# check that the order is correct:
all(vapply(1:nrow(dall), function(i) dall$jtyp[i] == crea1FA.pred$jtyp[i] && dall$liking[i] == crea1FA.pred$liking[i] && 
             dall$vivid[i] == crea1FA.pred$vivid[i] && dall$origin[i] == crea1FA.pred$origin[i] && dall$aesth[i] == crea1FA.pred$aesth[i] && 
             dall$curios[i] == crea1FA.pred$curios[i], logical(1)));
dall$crea <- crea1FA.pred$crea;
dall$crea <- 1 + (7-1)*(dall$crea - min(dall$crea, na.rm=TRUE))/(max(dall$crea, na.rm=TRUE) - min(dall$crea, na.rm=TRUE)); # convert it to a 1 to 7 scale to make it directly comparable with the original scales
```

```{r fig.width=2*5, fig.height=2*5, fig.cap=capFig("Distribution of *creativity* scores (1FA model by judge type) by judge type and subcategory of drawing."), message=FALSE}
grid.arrange(ggplot(dall, aes(x=crea)) + 
               geom_histogram(color="black", alpha=0.5) +
               scale_fill_discrete(name="Cluster") + theme_bw() + ggtitle("All judges and categories") + theme(legend.position="bottom"),
             ggplot(dall, aes(x=crea)) + 
               geom_histogram(aes(fill=jtyp), color="black", alpha=0.5) + facet_grid(. ~ jtyp) +
               scale_fill_manual(values=c("human"="steelblue", "AI"="orange", "gAI"="red"), name="Judge") + theme_bw()  + ggtitle("By judge across categories") + theme(legend.position="bottom"),
             ggplot(dall, aes(x=crea)) + 
               geom_histogram(aes(fill=jtyp), color="black", alpha=0.5) + facet_grid(jtyp ~ cat) +
               scale_fill_manual(values=c("human"="steelblue", "AI"="orange", "gAI"="red"), name="Judge") + theme_bw() + ggtitle("By judge and category") + theme(legend.position="bottom"),
             ggplot(dall, aes(x=crea)) + 
               geom_histogram(aes(fill=jtyp), color="black", alpha=0.5) + facet_grid(jtyp ~ subcat) +
               scale_fill_manual(values=c("human"="steelblue", "AI"="orange", "gAI"="red"), name="Judge") + theme_bw() + ggtitle("By judge and subcategory") + theme(legend.position="bottom"),
             ncol=2);
```

```{r fig.width=2*5, fig.height=1*5, fig.cap=capFig("Distribution of *creativity* scores (1FA model by judge type) by judge type and subcategory of drawing."), message=FALSE}
ggplot(dall, aes(y=crea, x=subcat)) + 
  geom_jitter(aes(x=subcat), alpha=0.1, color="gray80") +
  geom_boxplot(aes(fill=subcat), color="black", alpha=0.25) + facet_grid(. ~ jtyp) +
  geom_violin(aes(fill=subcat), color="blue", alpha=0.10) + facet_grid(. ~ jtyp) +
  scale_fill_viridis_d(name="subcat") + theme_bw() + ggtitle("By judge and subcategory") + theme(legend.position="bottom");
```


## Interim conclusions

The cluster analysis, the correlation matrix, the PCA and (E/C)FA seem to suggest that:

1. human and AI judgments are qualitatively different, with the guided AI ones somewhat "in between", but
2. that a 1 factor model, "creativity", captures the main aspects of the 5-questions scores, especially for the human and guided AI judges, but
3. this 1FA model has different scores for the three types of judge.

Therefore, we will proceed with a unified analysis where we use the predicted score on this latent "creativity" (*crea*) -- estimated differently for the human, guided AI, and AI judges -- as the DV of interest.


# Inter-rater agreement

As our design does not have the human judges judging all items, but only a subset of the items in such a way that all items are judged by multiple (but different) judges, the usual inter-rater agreement measures cannot be easily applied.
However, we can capture how similar various items are considered to be by looking at their variance (standard deviation) of their rating across the judges that did judge them.

```{r }
# compute the standard deviation of the ratings (poor man's proxy for inter-rate agreement):
dall.long <- dall[,c("iid", "cat", "subcat", "jtyp", "liking", "vivid", "origin", "aesth", "curios", "crea")] %>% melt(measure.vars=c("liking", "vivid", "origin", "aesth", "curios", "crea"), variable.name="measure", value.name="value");
dvariance <- dall.long %>% group_by(iid, measure, jtyp) %>% mutate("mean"=mean(value), "sd"=sd(value)) %>% dplyr::select(iid, measure, mean, sd, cat, subcat, jtyp) %>% unique();
```
```{r fig.width=2*6, fig.height=1*5, fig.cap=capFig("Stdandard deviation (sd) of scores for each scale, including the latent creativity (*crea*), by judge type across categories."), message=FALSE}
ggplot(dvariance, aes(x=sd, fill=jtyp)) + 
  geom_density(alpha=0.25) + 
  facet_grid(. ~ measure) +
  scale_fill_manual(values=c("human"="steelblue", "AI"="orange", "gAI"="red"), name="Judge") + theme_bw()  + ggtitle("StdDev of scores by judge type across categories") + theme(legend.position="bottom");
```
```{r fig.width=2*6, fig.height=1*5, fig.cap=capFig("Stdandard deviation (sd) of scores for each scale, including the latent creativity (*crea*), by judge type and scategory."), message=FALSE}
ggplot(dvariance, aes(x=sd, fill=jtyp)) + 
  geom_density(alpha=0.25) + 
  facet_grid(subcat ~ measure) +
  scale_fill_manual(values=c("human"="steelblue", "AI"="orange", "gAI"="red"), name="Judge") + theme_bw()  + ggtitle("StdDev of scores bt judge and subcategory") + theme(legend.position="bottom");
```

It seems that:

- the patterns are *very similar across measures*, including the latent creativity score,
- overall, the *consistency is quite good* across all items, measures and judge types (mean sd across measures `r round(mean(dvariance$sd[ dvariance$measure != "crea" ], na.rm=TRUE),2)`),
- AI is very consistent in its judgments (`r round(mean(dvariance$sd[ dvariance$measure != "crea" & dvariance$jtyp == "AI" ], na.rm=TRUE),2)`),
- humans show the most spread (`r round(mean(dvariance$sd[ dvariance$measure != "crea" & dvariance$jtyp == "human" ], na.rm=TRUE),2)`),
- guidedAI is in between (`r round(mean(dvariance$sd[ dvariance$measure != "crea" & dvariance$jtyp == "gAI" ], na.rm=TRUE),2)`).


# What influences "creativity"?

Given that this creativity score is inherently bounded (as are its indicators), we will normalize it **between 0 (minimum) and 1 (maximum)** and use *beta regression* whenever possible.
One extra complication concerns the values of exactly 0.0 and exactly 1.0, as standard beta regression cannot deal with them: there are two possible solutions: replace them by values that are very close to 0.0 and 1.0, respectively (say, 10^-7^ and 1-10^-7^) or use zero-and-one-inflated beta models: we opt here for the first as it requires less parameters and is simpler to interpret.

```{r}
pre4beta <- function(x, epsilon=1e-7)
{
  x.range <- range(x, na.rm=TRUE); x.min <- x.range[1]; x.max <- x.range[2]; x.range <- (x.max - x.min);
  if( x.max == x.min ) return (x);
  return (ifelse(x - x.min < epsilon, epsilon, ifelse(x.max - x < epsilon, 1-epsilon, (x - x.min)/x.range)));
}
dall$creab <- pre4beta(dall$crea);
```


## Intercept-only model

```{r}
if( !file.exists("./cache/glmm_c0.rds") )
{
  c.0 <- glmmTMB(creab ~ 1 + (1 | iid) + (1 | jid), data=dall, family=beta_family);
  saveRDS(c.0, file="./cache/glmm_c0.rds", compress="xz");
} else
{
  c.0 <- readRDS("./cache/glmm_c0.rds");
}
summary(c.0);
icc(c.0); # 91.5%
round(plogis(fixef(c.0)$cond["(Intercept)"]),2); # the expected creativity score, significantly lower than 0.50
```


## Judge type

```{r}
if( !file.exists("./cache/glmm_c1.rds") )
{
  c.1 <- update(c.0, . ~ . + jtyp);
  saveRDS(c.1, file="./cache/glmm_c1.rds", compress="xz");
} else
{
  c.1 <- readRDS("./cache/glmm_c1.rds");
}
summary(c.1);
anova(c.1, c.0); # judge type does not make a difference (good, as the latents are estimated taking the judge type into account)
```

The judge type by itself does not seem to make a difference (which makes perfect sense, given that we estimate the latent *crea* using judge-type-specific loadings)...


## Category

```{r }
if( !file.exists("./cache/glmm_c2.rds") )
{
  c.2 <- update(c.1, . ~ . + jtyp * cat);
  #summary(c.2);
  #anova(c.2, c.1); # c.2 is significantly better
  c.2a <- update(c.2, . ~ . - jtyp:cat);
  #anova(c.2a, c.2); # c.2a is worse -> interaction matters
  saveRDS(c.2, file="./cache/glmm_c2.rds", compress="xz");
} else
{
  c.2 <- readRDS("./cache/glmm_c2.rds");
}
summary(c.2);
anova(c.2, c.1); # c.2 is significantly better
```
```{r fig.width=5, fig.height=4, fig.cap=capFig("Predicted creativity score from a beta regression on the interaction between judge type and category of drawing while controlling (as random effects) for item and individual judge.")}
plot_model(c.2, type="pred", terms=c("jtyp", "cat")) + ylab("creativity") + ylim(0,1) + ggtitle("Predicted creativity");
```

... but it interacts significantly with the category, in that the human judges are clearly discriminating between the human- and AI-generated drawings, guided AI shows  atendency towards doing it, but the AI judges do not seem to care...


## Subcategory

```{r }
if( !file.exists("./cache/glmm_c3.rds") )
{
  c.3 <- update(c.1, . ~ . + jtyp * subcat);
  #summary(c.3);
  #anova(c.3, c.1); # c.3 is better
  c.3a <- update(c.3, . ~ . - jtyp:subcat);
  #anova(c.3a, c.3); # c.3a is worse -> interaction matters
  saveRDS(c.3, file="./cache/glmm_c3.rds", compress="xz");
} else
{
  c.3 <- readRDS("./cache/glmm_c3.rds");
}
summary(c.3);
anova(c.3, c.1); # c.3 is better
```
```{r fig.width=5, fig.height=4, fig.cap=capFig("Predicted creativity score from a beta regression on the interaction between judge type and subcategory of drawing while controlling (as random effects) for item and individual judge.")}
plot_model(c.3, type="pred", terms=c("jtyp", "subcat")) + ylab("creativity") + ylim(0,1) + ggtitle("Predicted creativity");
```

... and it interacts significantly with the subcategory, in that the human judges are clearly discriminating between them, with HVA >> HNA ≳ HAI >> SAI, guided AI do not show statistically significant differences but do seem to follow the human pattern, while the AI judges do not seem to care...


## Does the stimulus matter?

```{r }
if( !file.exists("./cache/glmm_c4.rds") )
{
  c.4 <- update(c.3, . ~ . + jtyp * stim + subcat * stim);
  #summary(c.4);
  #anova(c.4, c.3); # c.4 is better
  # let's see what really matters:
  c.4a <- update(c.4, . ~ . - subcat:stim); anova(c.4a, c.4); # c.4a is worse -> this interaction matters
  c.4b <- update(c.4a, . ~ . - jtyp:stim); anova(c.4b, c.4a); # c.4b is worse -> this interaction matters
  saveRDS(c.4, file="./cache/glmm_c4.rds", compress="xz");
} else
{
  c.4 <- readRDS("./cache/glmm_c4.rds");
}
summary(c.4);
anova(c.4, c.3); # c.4 is better
```
```{r fig.width=10, fig.height=6, fig.cap=capFig("Predicted creativity score from a beta regression on the interaction between judge type, subcategory of drawing, and sitmulus used to prompt the drawing, while controlling (as random effects) for item and individual judge.")}
plot_model(c.4, type="pred", terms=c("jtyp", "subcat", "stim")) + ylab("creativity") + ylim(0,1) + ggtitle("Predicted creativity");
```

... it does, but the effect is quite small (even if statistically significant) + there are convergence issues...


## Human judge characteristics

Just for the human judges, do their characteristics matter?

```{r }
if( !file.exists("./cache/glmm_h5.rds") )
{
  # computationally expensive, so better to cache the final model:
  h.0 <- glmmTMB(creab ~ 1 + (1 | iid) + (1 | jid), data=droplevels(dall[ dall$jtyp=="human" & !is.na(dall$jage) & !is.na(dall$jgen) & !is.na(dall$jeduc) & !is.na(dall$jasc), ]), family=beta_family);
  summary(h.0);
  icc(h.0); # 84.8%
  round(plogis(fixef(h.0)$cond["(Intercept)"]),2); # the expected creativity score, significantly lower than 0.50
  
  h.1 <- update(h.0, . ~ . + subcat * stim);
  summary(h.1);
  anova(h.1, h.0); # h.1 is better
  
  h.2 <- update(h.1, . ~ . + jage + jgen + jeduc + jasc + jage:subcat + jgen:subcat + jeduc:subcat + jasc:subcat);
  summary(h.2);
  anova(h.2, h.1); # h.2 is better, but only jasc seems to matter...
  
  h.3 <- update(h.2, . ~ . - jage:subcat - jgen:subcat - jeduc:subcat - jasc:subcat);
  summary(h.3);
  anova(h.3, h.2); # h3 is worse -> some of these interactions do matter: let's see which one(s):
  h.3a <- update(h.2, . ~ . - jage:subcat); anova(h.3a, h.2);  # h.3a is worse -> this interaction matters
  h.3b <- update(h.2, . ~ . - jgen:subcat); anova(h.3b, h.2);  # h.3b is better -> this interaction does not matter
  h.3c <- update(h.2, . ~ . - jeduc:subcat); anova(h.3c, h.2); # h.3c is better -> this interaction does not matter
  h.3d <- update(h.2, . ~ . - jasc:subcat); anova(h.3d, h.2);  # h.3d is equal/slightly better -> this interaction does not matter
  # -> the only interaction that seems to matter is age:
  h.4 <- update(h.2, . ~ . - jgen:subcat - jeduc:subcat - jasc:subcat); 
  summary(h.4);
  anova(h.4, h.2); # equivalent, as expected
  
  h.5 <- update(h.4, . ~ . - jgen - jeduc); # jage must stay because of the jage:subcat interaction
  summary(h.5);
  anova(h.5, h.4); # equivalent
  anova(h.5, h.1); # h.5 is still a better fit
  
  saveRDS(h.5, file="./cache/glmm_h5.rds", compress="xz");
} else
{
  h.5 <- readRDS("./cache/glmm_h5.rds");
}
```
```{r fig.width=4*2, fig.height=3*2, fig.cap=capFig("Predicted creativity score from a beta regression on for the human judges only is affected by the judge's AReA Score."), warning=FALSE, message=FALSE}
plot_model(h.5, type="pred", terms=c("jasc", "subcat", "stim")) + ylab("creativity") + ylim(0,1) + xlab("Judge's AReA score") + ggtitle("Predicted creativity (human judges only)");
```

... yes, mainly their AReA Score: the higher the score, the higher their creativity ratings.


## Human drawer characteristics

Just for the human drawers, do their characteristics matter?

```{r}
if( !file.exists("./cache/glmm_d4.rds") )
{
  # computationally expensive, so better to cache the final model:
  d.0 <- glmmTMB(creab ~ 1 + (1 | iid) + (1 | jid), data=dall[ dall$cat=="H" & !is.na(dall$dage) & !is.na(dall$dgen) & !is.na(dall$deduc) & !is.na(dall$dasc), ], family=beta_family); # artistic field is a bit messy so we leave it out for now...
  summary(d.0);
  icc(d.0); # 93.6%
  round(plogis(fixef(d.0)$cond["(Intercept)"]),2); # the expected creativity score is close to 0.50
  
  d.1 <- update(d.0, . ~ . + jtyp * subcat);
  summary(d.1);
  anova(d.1, d.0); # d.1 is better
  d.1a <- update(d.1, . ~ . - jtyp:subcat); anova(d.1a, d.1); #  d.1a is worse -> the interaction matters
  
  d.2 <- update(d.1, . ~ . + jtyp * stim);
  summary(d.2);
  anova(d.2, d.1); # d.2 is better
  
  d.3 <- update(d.2, . ~ . + dage + dgen + deduc + dasc + dage:subcat + dgen:subcat + deduc:subcat + dasc:subcat);
  summary(d.3);
  anova(d.3, d.2); # d.3 is better: let's see which new interactions and main effects matter:
  
  d.3a <- update(d.3, . ~ . - dage:subcat);  anova(d.3a, d.3); # d.3a is worse -> this interaction matters
  d.3b <- update(d.3, . ~ . - dgen:subcat);  anova(d.3b, d.3); # d.3b is worse -> this interaction matters
  d.3c <- update(d.3, . ~ . - deduc:subcat); anova(d.3c, d.3); # d.3c is worse -> this interaction matters
  d.3d <- update(d.3, . ~ . - dasc:subcat);  anova(d.3d, d.3); # they are pretty much equivalent -> this interaction does not really matter
  d.3e <- update(d.3d, . ~ . - dasc);  anova(d.3e, d.3); # they are pretty equivalent -> dasc does not matter
  
  # so, the best model so far is:
  d.4 <- update(d.3, . ~ . - dasc - dasc:subcat); 
  summary(d.4); 
  anova(d.4, d.3); # d.3 a tiny bit better but not seriously so...
  
  saveRDS(d.4, file="./cache/glmm_d4.rds", compress="xz");
} else
{
  d.4 <- readRDS("./cache/glmm_d4.rds");
}
```

```{r fig.width=4*2, fig.height=3*2, fig.cap=capFig("Predicted creativity score from a beta regression on for the human dawers only."), warning=FALSE, message=FALSE}
plot_model(d.4, type="pred", terms=c("jtyp", "subcat", "stim")) + ylab("creativity") + ylim(0,1) + xlab("Judge type") + ggtitle("Predicted creativity (human drawers only)");
```

```{r fig.width=5, fig.height=4, fig.cap=capFig("Predicted creativity score from a beta regression on for the human dawers only."), warning=FALSE, message=FALSE}
plot_model(d.4, type="pred", terms=c("dage", "subcat", "dgen")) + ylab("creativity") + ylim(0,1) + xlab("Drawer's age") + ggtitle("Predicted creativity (human drawers only)");
```

```{r fig.width=5, fig.height=4, fig.cap=capFig("Predicted creativity score from a beta regression on for the human dawers only."), warning=FALSE, message=FALSE}
plot_model(d.4, type="pred", terms=c("dage", "subcat", "deduc")) + ylab("creativity") + ylim(0,1) + xlab("Drawer's age") + ggtitle("Predicted creativity (human drawers only)");
```

So, it seems that for the human drawers, being a professional, being older and having less education (?) increases the creativity ratings.

Let's focus now on (a) the visual artists (they have more info) and (b) on the human raters.


### The visual artists

```{r}
if( !file.exists("./cache/glmm_da4.rds") )
{
  # computationally expensive, so better to cache the final model:
  da.0 <- glmmTMB(creab ~ 1 + (1 | iid) + (1 | jid), data=dall[ dall$cat=="H" & dall$subcat=="HVA" & !is.na(dall$dage) & !is.na(dall$dgen) & !is.na(dall$deduc) & !is.na(dall$dasc) & !is.na(dall$dart), ], family=beta_family); # artistic field is a bit messy so we leave it out for now...
  summary(da.0);
  icc(da.0); # 94.8%
  round(plogis(fixef(da.0)$cond["(Intercept)"]),2); # the expected creativity score is close to 0.50
  
  da.1 <- update(da.0, . ~ . + jtyp);
  summary(da.1);
  anova(da.1, da.0); # judge type does not matter
  
  da.2 <- update(da.0, . ~ . + jtyp * stim);
  summary(da.2);
  anova(da.2, da.0); # da.2 is better
  
  da.3 <- update(da.2, . ~ . + dage + dgen + deduc + dasc + dart);
  summary(da.3);
  anova(da.3, da.2); # da.3 is better: let's see which new interactions and main effects matter:
  
  da.3a <- update(da.3, . ~ . - dage);  anova(da.3a, da.3); # da.3a is worse -> this matters
  da.3b <- update(da.3, . ~ . - dgen);  anova(da.3b, da.3); # da.3b is worse -> this matters
  da.3c <- update(da.3, . ~ . - deduc); anova(da.3c, da.3); # da.3c is worse -> this matters
  da.3d <- update(da.3, . ~ . - dasc);  anova(da.3d, da.3); # they are pretty much equivalent -> this does not really matter
  da.3e <- update(da.3, . ~ . - dart);  anova(da.3e, da.3); # they are pretty much equivalent -> this does not really matter
  
  # so, the best model so far is:
  da.4 <- update(da.3, . ~ . - dasc - dart); 
  summary(da.4); 
  anova(da.4, da.3); # indeed
  
  saveRDS(da.4, file="./cache/glmm_da4.rds", compress="xz");
} else
{
  da.4 <- readRDS("./cache/glmm_da4.rds");
}
```

```{r fig.width=4*2, fig.height=3*2, fig.cap=capFig("Predicted creativity score from a beta regression on for the visual artists only."), warning=FALSE, message=FALSE}
plot_model(da.4, type="pred", terms=c("dage", "jtyp", "stim")) + ylab("creativity") + ylim(0,1) + xlab("Age") + ggtitle("Predicted creativity (visual artists only)");
```

```{r fig.width=9, fig.height=4, fig.cap=capFig("Predicted creativity score from a beta regression on for the visual artists only."), warning=FALSE, message=FALSE}
plot_model(da.4, type="pred", terms=c("jtyp", "dgen", "deduc")) + ylab("creativity") + ylim(0,1) + xlab("Judge") + ggtitle("Predicted creativity (visual artists only)");
```

So, for the visual artists, the AReA score and the number of years of processional work seem to not matter, while being younger, female, and having an MA increases the creativity scores.



# Conclusions

- there seems to be a *qualitative* difference between human and AI judges, with the guided AI judges somewhere in between,
- the *human judgments* and the *guided AI judgments* on the 5 questions seem to be captured relatively well by a *single factor*, while the *AI judgments* seem to be better captured by a *2-factor model*,
- when using a 1FA model for the human, AI and guided AI judgments (so we can meaningfully combine and compare them, while still allowing different loadings for each of them), we find the same qualitative difference, with the *human judges* being very sensitive to the *category and subcategory* of drawing, while the AI is much less so (if at all), and the guided AI partially mimicking the human judgments but with too much noise for any clear-cut pattern to emerge,
- in particular, the *human judges* seem to score significantly higher the drawing by visual artists, followed by those by naive humans and, very closely, by those by human-inspired AI, with the lowest scores given to the self-generated AI drawings (i.e., *HVA ≫ HNA ≳ HAI ≫ SAI*), with the guided AI showing a similar trend (but completely masked by noise),
- the *human judges*' judgments are positively affected by the judge's own *AReA score*,
- for the *human drawers*, being a professional, being older and having less education increases the creativity ratings,
- however, focusing on the human *visual artists* suggest that being younger, female, and having an MA increases the creativity scores,
- the particular initial *stimulus* used to generate a drawing statically matters, but, in practice, the differences are very small.


# Session information

```{r warning=FALSE, results='asis'}
if( require(benchmarkme) ) 
{
  # CPU:
  cpu_info <- benchmarkme::get_cpu();
  if( is.null(cpu_info) )
  {
    cat("**CPU:** unknown.\n\n");
  } else
  {
    if( !is.null(cpu_info$model_name) && !is.na(cpu_info$model_name) )
    {
      cat(paste0("**CPU:** ",cpu_info$model_name));
      if( !is.null(cpu_info$no_of_cores) && !is.na(cpu_info$no_of_cores) )
      {
        cat(paste0(" (",cpu_info$no_of_cores," threads)"));
      }
      cat("\n\n");
    } else
    {
      cat("**CPU:** unknown.\n\n");
    }
  }
  
  # RAM:
  ram_info <- benchmarkme::get_ram();
  if( is.null(ram_info) || is.na(ram_info) )
  {
    cat("**RAM (memory):** unknown.\n\n");
  } else
  {
    cat("**RAM (memory):** "); print(ram_info); cat("\n");
  }
} else
{
  cat("**RAM (memory):** cannot get info (try installing package 'benchmarkme').\n\n");
}
```

```{r}
pander::pander(sessionInfo());
```

```{r}
if( exists("start_time_force_compute_all") )
{
  cat(paste0("\n\n**Note: the full computation took ", round(difftime(Sys.time(), start_time_force_compute_all, units="mins"), 2), " minutes on this machine.**\n\n"))
}

```


# Bibliography
